draft.introduction: The mysteries of the Great Gods at Samothrace (active c. 7th century BCE–4th century CE) were among the most prestigious initiations in the ancient world. Unlike Eleusis, Samothracian initiation was open to all regardless of language or status, including slaves.

translation.original_ref: Scholia on Apollonius Rhodius, Argonautica I.917
translation.text: Those who have been initiated at Samothrace are thought to be preserved from dangers at sea. For the gods of Samothrace are guardians of those who sail. The initiation consists of two grades: the mystes and the epoptes, and it is lawful for all persons to be initiated.
translation.original_text: οἱ μυηθέντες ἐν Σαμοθράκῃ δοκοῦσιν ἐκ τῶν κατὰ θάλασσαν κινδύνων σώζεσθαι

translator_notes.note: The Samothracian mysteries were unusual in their openness: no restrictions of sex, status, or ethnicity applied. This universalism parallels the Pauline claim that in Christ “there is neither slave nor free” (Galatians 3:28), and may reflect a common cultural movement toward inclusive salvation cults.
